1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)

71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.

141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. 152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Know your target in the dependency graph (leandag)
18	
19	The blueprint dependency graph is queryable **read-only** — `archon` is on PATH, `--json` is parseable (the banner goes to stderr). Use it to ground your work instead of guessing:
20	
21	- `archon dag-query node --node <label> --json` — your target's blueprint entry, its `\lean{}` pin, and status. **If the `\lean{}` pin is a `…TODO.…` placeholder, the Lean declaration does not exist yet** — you must *create* it (the typed `sorry`, or the proof if your mode builds it), not "fill" a name that isn't in the environment.
22	- `archon dag-query ancestors --node <label> --json` — your target's full dependency closure. Build on deps that are actually done (`\leanok`); a dependency still marked ∞ / `sorry` is **not** a sound foundation — cite it only if it genuinely typechecks today.
23	- `archon dag-query gaps --json` — the ∞ holes (statements with no informal proof). If your target's cone hits one, the blueprint sketch is incomplete: flag it in your task_result rather than inventing a proof to paper over it.
24	- `archon dag-query unmatched --json` — Lean decls with no blueprint entry. After you add helpers, your new names surface here until they're blueprinted — that is the debt you must report (see Logging → "Needs blueprint entry").
25	
26	The label↔Lean mapping is the `\lean{}` annotation in the blueprint block; `leandag stats` / `leandag focus` also give a project-wide view. You never *write* the graph — that's the dag/plan/review agents' job — but reading it keeps you from proving against a hollow or unfinished foundation.
27	
28	## Protected declarations
29	
30	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
31	
32	## Talking to the user (TO_USER.md)
33	
34	`.archon/TO_USER.md` is a *persistent* shared notice board surfaced to the user as a banner. You may add a bullet **only** for something the user genuinely must act on that you hit while proving — e.g. a missing credential/dependency a sorry is gated on, or a `/- USER: -/` question you cannot resolve. Discipline:
35	
36	- **Concise + relevant**: keep the whole file ≤ 2–3 short bullets; before adding, read it and delete any bullet no longer true. Never a question queue — the loop never waits.
37	- **Concurrency**: provers run in parallel (one per file). To avoid clobbering a sibling lane's bullet, append your single bullet prefixed with your file (`- [Foo/Bar.lean] …`) rather than rewriting the whole file, and only when it's truly user-actionable. Routine "couldn't close this sorry" notes go in `task_results/<your_file>.md`, NOT here.
38	
39	## Avoid early termination
40	
41	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
42	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
43	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
44	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
45	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
46	
47	## Task completion criteria
48	
49	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
50	
51	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
52	2. Zero axioms introduced.
53	3. The file compiles cleanly.
54	
55	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
56	
57	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
58	
59	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
60	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
61	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
62	
63	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
64	
65	## When infrastructure is missing
66	
67	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
68	
69	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
70	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
71	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
72	
73	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
74	
75	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
76	
77	## Proof style
78	
79	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
80	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
81	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
82	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
83	- **List every new declaration you introduce under a `## Needs blueprint entry` heading in your task_result** (name, file, and the facts its proof relies on) — see Logging below. The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: the review agent and planner consume this list (and `archon dag-query unmatched`) to give each new declaration a blueprint block. An unreported helper is an invisible, isolated dependency that silently corrupts the frontier — flagging it is mandatory, not optional. (You never write the blueprint yourself; you only name what needs one.)
84	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.

1	\chapter{The Grassmannian over $\mathbb{Z}$: affine charts and gluing}
2	\label{chap:Picard_GrassmannianCells}
3	% NOTE: no `% archon:covers` line — these GR-cells/GR-glue declarations are destined
4	% for their own Lean file AlgebraicJacobian/Picard/GrassmannianCells.lean (1:1 slug
5	% mapping), a deliberate split from QuotScheme.lean for parallel proving once QUOT-repr
6	% activates. QuotScheme.lean is covered solely by Picard_QuotScheme.tex.
7	
8	% STRATEGY NOTE. This chapter blueprints the FIRST TWO sub-phases (GR-cells,
9	% GR-glue) of the Grassmannian-scheme construction that ultimately underlies
10	% thm:grassmannian_representable in chapter Picard_QuotScheme. Following
11	% [Nitsure], \S 1 ("Construction of Grassmannian"), the scheme
12	% \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) is built directly by gluing
13	% \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)
14	% along explicit matrix-algebra transition maps, and shown smooth, separated
15	% and proper over \(\mathbb{Z}\). The later sub-phases (GR-quot: tautological
16	% quotient; GR-repr: functor-of-points; and the relative-over-\(S\) version
17	% with a vector bundle \(V\)) are OUT OF SCOPE here and are blueprinted with
18	% def:grassmannian_scheme / thm:grassmannian_representable in
19	% chapter Picard_QuotScheme. The declarations below are deliberately disjoint
20	% from that chapter's: this chapter adds only the construction lemmas for the
21	% absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\).
22	
23	\section{Set-up: charts indexed by $d$-subsets}
24	\label{sec:gr_setup}
25	
26	Fix integers \(r \ge d \ge 1\). For a \(d \times r\) matrix \(M\) over a ring
27	and a subset \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\), write \(M_I\)
28	for the \emph{\(I\)-th minor} of \(M\): the \(d \times d\) submatrix whose
29	columns are those indexed by \(I\). We construct the Grassmannian scheme
30	\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\) by gluing one affine chart \(U^I\) for
31	each such \(I\). The construction is, conceptually, the quotient
32	\(\mathrm{GL}_{d,\mathbb{Z}} \backslash V\) of the scheme \(V\) of rank-\(d\)
33	matrices, but we never use the language of group-scheme actions: the charts and
34	their transition maps are elementary matrix algebra over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Taking
35	\(d = 1\) recovers \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,1) = \mathbb{P}^{r-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\).
36	
37	\begin{definition}
38	[Affine chart \(U^I\)]
39	  \label{def:gr_affine_chart}
40	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.affineChart}
41	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], "Construction of Hilbert and Quot Schemes", \S 1,
42	  % sub-section "Construction by gluing together affine patches" (read from
43	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L807-L821).
44	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
45	  % "If $M$ is a $d\times r$-matrix, and
46	  %  $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with cardinality $\#(I)$ equal
47	  %  to $d$, the $I$ th minor $M_I$ of $M$ will
48	  %  mean the $d\times d$ minor of $M$ whose columns are indexed by
49	  %  $I$.
50	  %
51	  %  For any subset $I\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(I) =d$,
52	  %  consider the $d\times r$ matrix $X^I$ whose $I$ the minor
53	  %  $X^I_I$ is the $d\times d$ identity matrix $1_{d\times d}$, while the remaining
54	  %  entries of $X^I$ are independent variables $x^I_{p,q}$ over $\Z$.
55	  %  Let $\Z[X^I]$ denote the polynomial ring in the variables
56	  %  $x^I_{p,q}$, and let $U^I = \spec \Z[X^I]$, which is non-canonically
57	  %  isomorphic to the affine space $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$."
58	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
59	  Let \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) with \(\#(I) = d\). Let \(X^I\) denote the
60	  \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(I\)-th minor \(X^I_I\) is the \(d \times d\)
61	  identity matrix \(1_{d \times d}\) and whose remaining \(d(r - d)\) entries are
62	  independent indeterminates \(x^I_{p,q}\) (\(1 \le p \le d\),
63	  \(q \in \{1,\dots,r\} \setminus I\)) over \(\mathbb{Z}\). Write
64	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) for the polynomial ring in these \(d(r-d)\) variables and
65	  set
66	  \[
67	    U^I \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I].
68	  \]
69	  The choice of an ordering of the free entries gives a (non-canonical)
70	  isomorphism \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\); in particular
71	  \(U^I\) is a smooth affine \(\mathbb{Z}\)-scheme of relative dimension
72	  \(d(r-d)\). Intuitively a \(\mathbb{Z}\)-point of \(U^I\) is a \(d \times r\)
73	  matrix whose \(I\)-columns form the identity, i.e.\ a rank-\(d\) quotient of
74	  \(\mathbb{Z}^{r}\) trivialised on the coordinates \(I\).
75	\end{definition}
76	
77	\section{Transition maps and the cocycle condition}
78	\label{sec:gr_transition}
79	
80	\begin{definition}
81	[Transition map \(\theta_{I,J}\)]
82	  \label{def:gr_transition}
83	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionMap}
84	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart}
85	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
86	  % affine patches" (read from
87	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L822-L836).
88	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
89	  % "For any $J\subset \{1,\ldots, r\}$ with $\#(J) =d$, let
90	  %  $P^I_J = \det(X^I_J) \in \Z[X^I]$
91	  %  where $X^I_J$ is the $J$ th minor of $X^I$.
92	  %  Let $U^I_J = \spec \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$
93	  %  the open subscheme of $U^I$ where $P^I_J$ is invertible. This means
94	  %  the $d\times d$-matrix $X^I_J$ admits an inverse $(X^I_J)^{-1}$
95	  %  on $U^I_J$.
96	  %
97	  %  For any $I$ and $J$, a ring homomorphism
98	  %  $\theta_{I,J} : \Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \to \Z[X^I, 1/P^I_J]$ is
99	  %  defined as follows. The images of the variables
100	  %  $x^J_{p,q}$ are given by the entries of the matrix formula
101	  %  $\theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J) ^{-1}\,X^I$.
102	  %  In particular, we have $\theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = 1/P^I_J$,
103	  %  so the map extends to $\Z[X^J, 1/P^J_I]$."
104	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
105	  For \(I, J\) of cardinality \(d\), set
106	  \(P^I_J := \det(X^I_J) \in \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\), the determinant of the \(J\)-th
107	  minor of \(X^I\), and let
108	  \[
109	    U^I_J \;:=\; \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]
110	  \]
111	  be the principal open subscheme of \(U^I\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) on
112	  which \(P^I_J\) is invertible; over \(U^I_J\) the matrix \(X^I_J\) is
113	  invertible, with inverse \((X^I_J)^{-1}\) whose entries lie in
114	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) by Cramer's rule. Note \(P^I_I = 1\), so
115	  \(U^I_I = U^I\). The \emph{transition map} \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the ring
116	  homomorphism
117	  \[
118	    \theta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I] \longrightarrow
119	    \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
120	    \qquad
121	    \theta_{I,J}(X^J) = (X^I_J)^{-1}\, X^I,
122	  \]
123	  i.e.\ the indeterminate \(x^J_{p,q}\) maps to the \((p,q)\)-entry of the
124	  \(d \times r\) matrix \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). This is well defined: the
125	  \(J\)-minor of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_J = 1_{d\times d}\),
126	  matching the identity \(J\)-block of \(X^J\), and the \(I\)-minor of
127	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\), so
128	  \[
129	    \theta_{I,J}(P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J
130	  \]
131	  is invertible in the target; hence \(\theta_{I,J}\) extends from
132	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]\) to the localisation \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J, 1/P^J_I]\). Dually,
133	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) is the comorphism of a morphism of schemes
134	  \(U^I_J \to U^J_I\), which we also denote \(\theta_{I,J}\); it sends the
135	  matrix \(X^I\) to \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). One has \(\theta_{I,I} = \id_{U^I}\).
136	\end{definition}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}
139	[Cocycle condition for the transition maps]
140	  \label{lem:gr_cocycle}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.cocycleCondition}
142	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart}
143	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
144	  % affine patches" (read from
145	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L838-L848).
146	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
147	  % "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
148	  %  we leave it to the reader to verify that
149	  %  for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
150	  %  cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
151	  %  $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
152	  %  is satisfied."
153	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
154	  For any three subsets \(I, J, K \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\) of cardinality
155	  \(d\), the transition maps of \cref{def:gr_transition} satisfy the cocycle
156	  condition. As morphisms of schemes, on the triple overlap inside \(U^I\),
157	  \[
158	    \theta_{I,K} \;=\; \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J},
159	  \]
160	  equivalently, as ring homomorphisms,
161	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\) (the two read the same
162	  identity through the order-reversal of \(\Spec\)). Together with
163	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\), this is exactly the compatibility datum required to
164	  glue the charts.
165	\end{lemma}
166	
167	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Note that $\theta_{I,I}$ is identity on $U^I_I = U^I$, and
168	% we leave it to the reader to verify that
169	% for any three subsets $I$, $J$ and $K$ of $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of
170	% cardinality $d$, the co-cycle condition
171	% $\theta_{I,K}= \theta_{I,J}\theta_{J,K}$
172	% is satisfied."  (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
173	% L838-L848.)
174	\begin{proof}
175	  \uses{def:gr_transition, def:gr_affine_chart}
176	  We verify the scheme-morphism form
177	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) by a direct matrix
178	  computation, valid over the open locus of \(U^I\) on which all the minors
179	  involved are invertible (namely \(P^I_J\) and \(P^I_K\), which suffices since
180	  the maps are determined by their restriction to a dense open and all rings are
181	  domains; the computation is an identity of matrices with entries in
182	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J, 1/P^I_K]\)).
183	
184	  Start from the matrix \(X^I\) (the universal point of \(U^I\)). By
185	  \cref{def:gr_transition},
186	  \[
187	    M^J \;:=\; \theta_{I,J}(X^I) \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
188	  \]
189	  a \(d \times r\) matrix whose \(J\)-minor is the identity, hence a point of
190	  \(U^J\). Its \(K\)-minor is
191	  \[
192	    M^J_K \;=\; (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_K,
193	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
194	    (M^J_K)^{-1} \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J,
195	  \]
196	  using \((AB)^{-1} = B^{-1} A^{-1}\) for the invertible \(d \times d\) matrices
197	  \(X^I_J\) and \(X^I_K\). Applying \(\theta_{J,K}\) to \(M^J\) gives
198	  \[
199	    \theta_{J,K}(M^J) \;=\; (M^J_K)^{-1} M^J
200	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I_J \,\cdot\, (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I
201	    \;=\; (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I,
202	  \]
203	  the middle product \(X^I_J (X^I_J)^{-1} = 1_{d\times d}\) cancelling by
204	  associativity of matrix multiplication. The right-hand side is precisely
205	  \(\theta_{I,K}(X^I) = (X^I_K)^{-1} X^I\). Thus
206	  \(\theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,K}\) agree on the
207	  universal matrix \(X^I\), hence agree as morphisms \(U^I \supseteq U^I_J \cap
208	  U^I_K \to U^K\). The special case \(K = I\) (resp.\ \(J = I\)) together with
209	  \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\) shows each \(\theta_{I,J}\) is an isomorphism onto its
210	  image with inverse \(\theta_{J,I}\), since
211	  \(\theta_{J,I} \circ \theta_{I,J} = \theta_{I,I} = \id\).
212	\end{proof}

214	\section{The glued Grassmannian scheme}
215	\label{sec:gr_glued}
216	
217	\begin{definition}
218	[The Grassmannian scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
219	  \label{def:gr_glued_scheme}
220	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.scheme}
221	  \uses{def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_transition, lem:gr_cocycle}
222	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Construction by gluing together
223	  % affine patches" (read from
224	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L843-L850).
225	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
226	  % "Therefore the schemes
227	  %  $U^I$, as $I$ varies over all the
228	  %  ${r\choose d}$ different
229	  %  subsets of  $\{ 1,\ldots, r\}$ of cardinality $d$,
230	  %  can be glued together by the co-cycle $(\theta_{I,J})$ to form a
231	  %  finite-type scheme $\grass(r,d)$ over $\Z$. As each $U^I$ is
232	  %  isomorphic to $\AA^{d(r-d)}_{\Z}$, it follows that
233	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is smooth of relative dimension $d(r-d)$."
234	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
235	  Gluing the affine charts \(\{U^I\}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}), one for each
236	  of the \(\binom{r}{d}\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) of cardinality
237	  \(d\), along the open subschemes \(U^I_J \subseteq U^I\) via the transition
238	  isomorphisms \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I_J \xrightarrow{\sim} U^J_I\)
239	  (\cref{def:gr_transition}) -- which satisfy the cocycle condition
240	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{J,K} \circ \theta_{I,J}\) and \(\theta_{I,I} = \id\)
241	  of \cref{lem:gr_cocycle} -- yields a scheme
242	  \[
243	    \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \longrightarrow \Spec \mathbb{Z}.
244	  \]
245	  It is of finite type over \(\mathbb{Z}\) (a finite gluing of finitely
246	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebras), and under the gluing each \(U^I\) is an
247	  open subscheme of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) with \(U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J\).
248	
249	  Moreover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is \emph{smooth of relative
250	  dimension \(d(r-d)\)}. Smoothness and relative dimension are local on the
251	  source; the charts \(\{U^I\}\) cover \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and each
252	  \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is
253	  affine space of dimension \(d(r-d)\) over \(\mathbb{Z}\), which is smooth over
254	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\) (the polynomial ring
255	  \(\mathbb{Z}[x_1, \dots, x_{d(r-d)}]\) is a smooth \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra). As
256	  the property may be checked on an open cover of the source, the conclusion
257	  follows.
258	\end{definition}

260	\section{Separatedness}
261	\label{sec:gr_separated}
262	
263	\begin{lemma}
264	[\(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)]
265	  \label{lem:gr_separated}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isSeparated}
267	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
268	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Separatedness" (read from
269	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860).
270	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
271	  % "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with $U^I\times U^J$
272	  %  can be seen to be the closed subscheme $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$
273	  %  defined by entries of the matrix formula $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$,
274	  %  and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
275	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
276	  The structure morphism \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
277	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is separated; equivalently, the diagonal
278	  \(\Delta : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \times_{\mathbb{Z}}
279	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a closed immersion.
280	\end{lemma}
281	
282	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "The intersection of the diagonal of $\grass(r,d)$ with
283	% $U^I\times U^J$ can be seen to be the closed subscheme
284	% $\Delta_{I,J}\subset U^I\times U^J$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
285	% $X^J_I\,X^I - X^J =0$, and so $\grass(r,d)$ is a separated scheme."
286	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L856-L860.)
287	\begin{proof}
288	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_transition}
289	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
290	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
291	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
292	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
293	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
294	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
295	  homomorphism is surjective.
296	
297	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
298	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
299	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
300	  \[
301	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
302	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
303	    \qquad
304	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,
305	  \]
306	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
307	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
308	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
309	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
310	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
311	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
312	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
313	  and in particular
314	  \[
315	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
316	  \]
317	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
318	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
319	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
320	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
321	  \[
322	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
323	  \]
324	  which on the diagonal holds because \(X^J = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) forces
325	  \(X^J_I = (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_I = (X^I_J)^{-1}\) and therefore \(X^J_I X^I =
326	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
327	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
328	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).
329	\end{proof}

337	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper}

358	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
359	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
360	  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
361	  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
362	  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
363	  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of
364	  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
365	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked
366	  %  separatedness of $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that
367	  %  $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
368	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
369	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)

372	
373	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is
374	% proper. It is enough to verify the valuative criterion of properness for
375	% discrete valuation rings. Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
376	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by a ring
377	% homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$. Having fixed one such $I$, next
378	% choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum, where
379	% $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes the discrete valuation. As
380	% $P^I_I = 1$, note that $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$
381	% and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$ lies in $GL_d(\K)$. Now consider the homomorphism
382	% $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
383	% $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$ Then $g$ defines the same morphism
384	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$, and moreover all $d\times d$ minors
385	% $X^J_K$ satisfy $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$. As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it
386	% follows from the above that in fact $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of
387	% $X^J$. Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ factors uniquely via
388	% $\Rr \subset \K$. The resulting morphism of schemes
389	% $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
390	% $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$. We have already checked separatedness of
391	% $\grass(r,d)$, so now we see that $\grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper."
392	% (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L865-L891.)
393	\begin{proof}
394	  \uses{def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_separated, def:gr_transition}
395	  Since \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is of finite type
396	  (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) and separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), and
397	  \(\mathbb{Z}\) is Noetherian, properness follows from the valuative criterion
398	  for discrete valuation rings: it suffices to show that every solid diagram
399	  \[
400	    \begin{array}{ccc}
401	      \Spec K & \xrightarrow{\ \varphi\ } & \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \\
402	      \downarrow & & \downarrow \pi \\
403	      \Spec R & \longrightarrow & \Spec \mathbb{Z}
404	    \end{array}
405	  \]
406	  with \(R\) a discrete valuation ring, \(K = \operatorname{Frac}(R)\) and
407	  valuation \(\nu : K \to \mathbb{Z} \cup \{\infty\}\), admits a unique diagonal
408	  filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
409	
410	  \emph{Existence.} The morphism \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) lands
411	  in some chart \(U^I\) (\(\Spec K\) has one point), so it is given by a ring map
412	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\). Among the \(\binom{r}{d}\) minors choose \(J\)
413	  with \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal. Because \(P^I_I = 1\) gives \(f(P^I_I) = 1\)
414	  with \(\nu = 0\), the minimum satisfies \(\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0\); in particular
415	  \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\) is invertible over
416	  \(K\). Define
417	  \[
418	    g : \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \longrightarrow K, \qquad
419	    g(X^J) = f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr) ;
420	  \]
421	  this is the composite of \(\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition}) with
422	  \(f\), so \(g\) defines the same \(K\)-point \(\varphi\), now exhibited as
423	  landing in \(U^J\).
424	
425	  We show every entry \(g(x^J_{p,q})\) lies in \(R\), i.e.\ has \(\nu \ge 0\).
426	  First, for any size-\(d\) subset \(K' \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\}\) the \(K'\)-minor
427	  of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) is \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\), so
428	  \[
429	    g(P^J_{K'}) = \det f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I_{K'}\bigr)
430	    = \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)},
431	    \qquad
432	    \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) = \nu(f(P^I_{K'})) - \nu(f(P^I_J)) \ge 0
433	  \]
434	  by minimality of \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\). Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\) is
435	  the identity, so by cofactor expansion each individual entry
436	  \(x^J_{p,q}\) (with \(q \notin J\)) equals, up to sign, the minor
437	  \(P^J_{K'}\) for \(K' = (J \setminus \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) -- the \(d \times d\)
438	  determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th identity column of \(X^J_J\) by
439	  the column \(q\). Hence \(\nu(g(x^J_{p,q})) = \nu(g(P^J_{K'})) \ge 0\) for all
440	  entries, and the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\). Therefore
441	  \(g(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]) \subseteq R\), so \(g\) factors uniquely as
442	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to R \hookrightarrow K\). The corresponding morphism
443	  \(\Spec R \to U^J \hookrightarrow \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) restricts to \(\varphi\)
444	  over \(\Spec K\) and lies over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\), providing the filler.
445	
446	  \emph{Uniqueness.} Any two fillers agree over the generic point \(\Spec K\)
447	  and, \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) being separated over \(\mathbb{Z}\)
448	  (\cref{lem:gr_separated}), two morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
449	  agreeing on the schematically dense open \(\Spec K\) coincide. This verifies
450	  the valuative criterion, so \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is
451	  proper.
452	\end{proof}

454	\section{Out of scope}
455	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
456	
457	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
458	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
459	and properness -- the GR-cells and GR-glue sub-phases. The following are
460	deliberately deferred to later sub-phases and are not treated here:
461	\begin{itemize}
462	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
463	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
464	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding (GR-quot).
465	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
